context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on participatory development, and I’m kinda stuck on the prompt. I think I get participation in a basic way, but idk how to organize the part about participation as both a goal and a method.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — that tension is actually one of the more interesting parts of the prompt. Before we dig into organization, let me ask: in your own words, what do you think is the difference between participation being a *goal* versus participation being a *method*? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and has identified a specific tension in the prompt — participation as goal vs. method. Rather than explaining this distinction, I should use a Socratic question to get them to articulate what they already understand about it. This will help me gauge their baseline and let them construct their own understanding. I'll keep it brief and targeted.

exchanges.student: I think as a goal means people having actual power is the outcome, and as a method means participation is used to make projects work better? I’m not fully sure if that distinction is too simple tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting intuition — you're on the right track. Now stress-test it a bit: can you think of a real situation where communities are *consulted* during a development project, but don't actually shape its outcome? Would that count as participation as a goal, a method, or neither?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a reasonable initial distinction. It's a good start but could be sharpened — particularly the 'method' side, which they themselves sense might be too simple. Rather than validating or correcting directly, I should push them to stress-test their own thinking with a concrete example, which will naturally deepen the distinction without me giving it to them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, like if villagers get asked for opinions on a water project but the agency already decided everything, that feels more like method than goal. Tbh it barely even feels like real participation.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and that instinct that it 'barely feels like real participation' is important. So here's the follow-up: if participation as a method can exist *without* participation as a goal, what does that tension actually mean for development projects? What's at stake when the two come apart?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a sharp observation — they're noticing that some forms of 'participation' may be more tokenistic than substantive. This is exactly the insight that connects to the tension in the prompt. I should affirm the direction and push them to name the tension more explicitly: what does it mean when method and goal diverge? This sets up the resolution question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think it means participation can get used kinda instrumentally, like to make a project look legit or run smoother, without changing power. What’s at stake is whether people are actually empowered or just being managed, but idk how to phrase that better.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good insight — hold onto it, it's the core of your argument there. On phrasing: that's your job, not mine! But you have the idea clearly in your head, so trust yourself to write it.

Let's move forward: the prompt asks whether this tension *can* be resolved. What do you think — is it possible to have participation that is both a genuine goal *and* an effective method at the same time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong conceptual insight about instrumental vs. transformative participation. They're asking for help phrasing it, but per the rules I must not suggest language or edit their writing. Instead, I should affirm the insight and redirect them toward the next part of the prompt — specifically the question of how this tension might be resolved — to keep the conversation moving productively toward a full response.

exchanges.student: I think maybe yes, but only if people have real influence over decisions and not just token input. Ngl I’m unsure if I should argue it’s fully resolvable or only partly.
exchanges.tutor: That uncertainty is actually productive — it means you're thinking carefully. But a reflection piece needs a clear argument. So ask yourself: what *conditions* would need to be in place for the tension to be fully resolved? And are those conditions realistic in most development contexts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with taking a clear position, which is good — it shows critical thinking. Rather than telling them which stance to take, I should push them to think about what evidence or conditions would support each position. This will help them commit to an argument based on reasoning rather than uncertainty, which is what a reflection piece requires.

exchanges.student: Maybe the conditions are shared decision-making, accountability, local capacity, and enough time/resources to include people for real. My guess is those aren’t usually fully present, so maybe the tension is only partly resolvable.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a defensible argument you can build on. You've got a lot of material now for the goal/method tension part. But the prompt also asks about two other things you haven't touched yet: the *structural factors* that made participation such a key element in development, and *how participation should be measured*. Which of those feels less clear to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a well-reasoned position on the resolution question. They've identified concrete conditions and drawn a realistic conclusion. This is good progress. We've now covered two of the four sub-questions in the prompt reasonably well. I should nudge them toward the remaining parts — specifically the structural factors that made participation central to development strategies, and the question of how participation is measured/judged — to ensure their reflection is complete.

exchanges.student: The structural factors part is fuzzier for me rn. I can guess stuff like state failure or top-down planning problems, but I’m not sure what counts as “structural” here.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts. Think historically: why did top-down, state-led development fall out of favor? What larger forces — economic, political, institutional — were shifting in the 1970s-90s that would have pushed development actors toward participation as an alternative?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a partial intuition about structural factors but needs to sharpen what 'structural' means in this context. Rather than defining it for them, I should ask a question that helps them think about why participation emerged historically as a response — this connects their intuition about top-down planning failure to broader systemic forces.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking debt crises, neoliberal reforms, weaker state capacity, and maybe criticism that big centralized plans ignored local knowledge. Also maybe donors/NGOs got more influential, but idk if I’m mixing too many things together.
exchanges.tutor: You're not mixing too much — those are all relevant. But instead of a list, can you see a *logic* connecting them? How do debt crises, weaker states, and donor influence all point in the same direction regarding participation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified several relevant structural factors and is now second-guessing whether they're mixing things together. This is a good sign of critical thinking. Rather than sorting it out for them, I should ask them to think about how these factors relate to each other — this will help them see a coherent narrative rather than a list, which is what a strong reflection needs. We're about 7 exchanges in, so I should also be thinking about pushing toward synthesis.

exchanges.student: Maybe the logic is that confidence in the state-led model dropped, so participation got framed as a fix for both legitimacy and effectiveness. Since states were weaker and donors/NGOs were stronger, development shifted toward local involvement as a way to reach people and justify projects.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong synthesis — you've essentially built your argument for that section. You now have material covering most of the prompt's questions. I'd encourage you to start drafting. What's your plan for organizing all of this into a coherent 700-800 word piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has synthesized the structural factors into a coherent narrative — this is excellent progress. We're close to the end of the planned conversation length and have covered most of the prompt's sub-questions. I should now push the student toward integrating everything into a written attempt, as per the redundancy/integration rule and because we're near the end of the exchange limit.

exchanges.student: I think intro + define participation, then a paragraph on ways it’s achieved, one on measuring/judging it, one on goal vs method, and one on structural factors. Can u help me check if that order makes sense, bc I don’t wanna make it feel random.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable draft structure. But rather than me checking it — ask yourself: does each section *build* toward your central argument about the goal/method tension, or does it feel like separate topics sitting next to each other? What's the thread connecting them all?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable structure but is asking me to validate it for them. Per the rules, I should not do the organizational work for them. Instead, I should ask them to evaluate their own structure by thinking about the logical flow — specifically whether the order builds toward an argument or just lists topics. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. No direct answers or submission-ready content were provided; instead, the tutor consistently prompted the student to generate and refine ideas independently. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student response before advancing. Communication was concise, warm, and appropriately formative.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave final answers or near-submission-ready content. Every substantive insight (goal vs. method distinction, instrumental participation, structural logic, structural factors) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. The tutor explicitly declined to do phrasing work for the student (Turn 4) and redirected the student to self-evaluate structure (Turn 10). No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., 'That's a solid starting intuition,' 'That's a strong synthesis') and moved forward only after validating prior steps. No diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback at key moments, such as noting that uncertainty is productive but a reflection piece needs a clear argument (Turn 5), and prompting the student to find a connecting logic rather than a list (Turn 8). 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily through all prompt components without repetition or off-topic drift. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive, tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented, and no grading language was used.
